retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bilirubin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As unconjugated bilirubin is always bound to albumin in serum, it cannot be filtered by the glomeruli (in the absence of glomerular disease). Thus, unconjugated bilirubin is never found in urine, even when an elevated level of unconjugated bilirubin is in circulation. Jaundice that occurs with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is termed acholuric because the urine is not darkened. Dark urine, however, occurs when there is an excretion of an excess of water-soluble, conjugated bilirubin. This is seen in conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and signifies the presence of either liver or biliary disease. Thus the presence of bilirubin in urine helps identify subtle hepatobiliary dysfunction leading to conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, even when the measured concentration of conjugated bilirubin in serum is only slightly elevated. An exception to this rule is when bilirubinuria is not detected in a patient with prolonged cholestasis and marked jaundice. This is due to the formation of delta bilirubin or conjugated bilirubin tightly bound to serum albumin. The absence of bilirubinuria in such patients should not cause any difficulty in diagnosing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, as the patient is jaundiced and serum-conjugated bilirubin is markedly elevated in such cases. [6] [7] [8] [9] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is a condition defined as elevated serum or plasma bilirubin (unconjugated) levels above the reference range of the laboratory. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia usually results from dysregulation in the bilirubin metabolism that includes increased production, impaired hepatic uptake, and decreased conjugation of bilirubin. In newborns, unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is very common, and increased bilirubin (unconjugated) levels can cause life-threatening kernicterus. This activity describes the pathophysiology and management of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. It also highlights the importance of teamwork in the management of patients with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The initial assessment of the patient presenting with jaundice depends on whether hyperbilirubinemia is the result of unconjugated or conjugated bilirubin. A urine test positive for bilirubin indicates conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Conjugated bilirubin is soluble in water; therefore, it can be excreted via urine but not unconjugated bilirubin due to water insolubility. Further, first-line laboratory testing should include measurement of the serum total bilirubin levels with fractionation (direct with indirect obtained by subtraction from total), followed by serum aspartate transaminase (AST), alanine transaminase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase, and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) levels, complete blood count (CBC) with an examination of the smear, a reticulocyte count, a direct Coombs test, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and haptoglobin levels. [29] Jaundice, with a toxic appearance, fever, or abnormal count of white blood cells indicates sepsis that requires prompt evaluation and adequate treatment.  The increased levels of AST and ALT are markers of hepatocyte damage/injury. However, increased levels of alkaline phosphatase and γ-glutamyltransferase suggest cholestasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Referrals are indicated in cases of unexplained and persistent elevation of bilirubin alone or combination with AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase; typically, total bilirubin greater than 3 mg/dL, aminotransferases two times and alkaline phosphatase 1.5 times the upper limit of normal are considered significant. Additionally, if multiple etiologies are suspected, specialist referral is advantageous.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Unconjugated (ie, indirect) hyperbilirubinemia (UHB) is the more common type and is either physiologic or pathologic. Physiologic jaundice accounts for 75% of neonatal hyperbilirubinemia and results from a physiologic alteration in neonatal bilirubin metabolism. Healthy adults have a total serum bilirubin (TSB) level of less than 1mg/dL. In neonates, normal TSB levels are comparatively higher, with age-dependant levels. Even healthy full-term newborns have an increased bilirubin load due to higher red blood cell (RBC) mass and decreased RBC lifespan. Metabolic bilirubin clearance is also compromised due to impaired activity of uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase (UGT), the enzyme needed for bilirubin conjugation. The activity level of the newborn UGT enzyme is approximately 1% that of an adult. [7] Moreover, neonates also have increased enterohepatic circulation, further contributing to elevated TSB levels. Physiologic jaundice typically appears in full-term infants 24 hours after birth, peaks at around 48 to 96 hours, and resolves by 2 to 3 weeks. [2] Conversely, pathologic unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia occurs within the first 24 hours after birth when the TSB level is >95% on age-specific bilirubin nomograms or increases ≥5 mg/dL/day or >0.2 mg/dL/hour. [8] Based on the mechanism of bilirubin elevation, the etiology of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia can be subdivided into the following 3 categories: increased bilirubin production, decreased bilirubin clearance, and miscellaneous.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Function Tests -- Pathophysiology -- Cholestasis Labs
retrieved_docs.content: Bilirubin is the end product of heme catabolism, with 80% derived from hemoglobin. Unconjugated bilirubin is transported to the liver loosely bound to albumin. Bilirubin is water-insoluble and cannot be excreted in the urine. Bilirubin that is conjugated is water-soluble and appears in the urine. It is conjugated in the liver to bilirubin glucuronide and subsequently secreted into bile and the gut, respectively. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum Bilirubin, Total and Direct : Jaundice, discoloration of body tissues, usually results from bilirubin levels greater than 3 mg/dl. Following identification of jaundice, unconjugated and conjugated fractions of bilirubin need to be obtained to distinguish between prehepatic and posthepatic jaundice. Elevation in unconjugated bilirubin indicates prehepatic or hepatic jaundice and may be managed medically; [12] whereas an increase in conjugated bilirubin suggests hepatocellular injury or cholestasis, which may necessitate bile duct surgery or therapeutic endoscopy. No consensus of direct bilirubin levels defining conjugated hyperbilirubinemia was encountered. The European Society for Pediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology, and Nutrition and the North American Society for Pediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology, and Nutrition deem direct bilirubin level above 1.0 mg/dL as anomalous when total bilirubin is elevated. In newborns, conjugated bilirubin of >= 0.5 mg/dL warrants infection workup; levels >=2 mg/dL requires more exhaustive hepatobiliary assessment. Total and direct bilirubin levels must be obtained in infants who are icteric after ages two weeks and three weeks in formula-fed and breast-fed infants, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the physiology of bilirubin metabolism. Explain how to differentiate unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Identify the several causes of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and summarize the available treatment options. Summarize the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: AST/ALT ratio is more than 2 to 1 in alcoholic liver disease. AST and ALT values are in 1000s; then the hepatocellular disease is likely due to toxins like acetaminophen or ischemia or viral. If ALP levels are five times elevated than normal and liver transaminases are normal or less than two times normal, then the most likely cause is biliary obstruction. The high serum ALP levels due to a biliary injury can be differentiated from bone disorders by ordering a GGT serum profile, increased levels confirm hepatic origin. If AST, ALT and ALP levels are normal- then the jaundice is not due to liver or bile duct injury. The cause must probably be pre-hepatic: inherited disorders of liver conjugation or blood disorders or defect in hepatic excretion (Rotor, Dubin-Johnson). Serum Bilirubin - whether there is a rise in unconjugated or conjugated bilirubin

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: As conjugated hyperbilirubinemia is a manifestation of a disease, rather than a disease itself, the prognosis varies depending on underlying disease conditions leading to conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Malignancy related to hepatobiliary system carries a poor prognosis; however, non-malignant causes like infections, toxins, non-malignant strictures tend to have a better outcome if treated promptly. Bilirubin levels correlate pointedly with short term mortality in some conditions, including primary biliary cholangitis and alcoholic hepatitis; nonetheless, the elevated bilirubin level does not contribute to the early mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST) and Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): AST and ALT serve as markers of hepatocellular injury. ALT increases are more specific to liver injury. Nonetheless, in chronic liver disease, these markers may be less significant as they may be normal or slightly elevated due to minimally remaining undamaged hepatic parenchymal cells. AST and ALT levels in 1000s are typically secondary to ischemia or liver toxins. Viral hepatitis may also yield ALTs in 1000s. AST to ALT ratio greater than or equal to 2:1 may suggest alcohol-induced liver damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Urine Bilirubin : The presence of bilirubin in urine suggests conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperbilirubinemia is a condition defined as elevated serum or plasma bilirubin levels above the reference range of the laboratory, and it is due to disorders of bilirubin metabolism. Depending on the form of bilirubin present in serum, hyperbilirubinemia can be further classified as unconjugated (indirect) or conjugated (direct). Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (albumin-bound) usually results from increased production, impaired hepatic uptake, and decreased conjugation of bilirubin. [1] [2] In neonates, jaundice typically occurs due to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, which is characterized by the increased levels of indirect or unconjugated bilirubin (UCB) in the serum. In newborns, the increased concentration of UCB can cross the blood-brain barrier, and deposit in the basal ganglia or cerebellum causing a bilirubin-induced encephalopathy or kernicterus [3] . Several inherited disorders can also produce unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, including Gilbert syndrome, Crigler-Najjar syndromes type I and II, and inherited disorders causing hemolytic anemia. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dysfunction in prehepatic phase results in elevated serum levels of unconjugated bilirubin while insult in post hepatic phase marks elevated conjugated bilirubin. Hepatic phase impairment can elevate both unconjugated and conjugated bilirubin. Increased urinary excretion of urobilinogen can be due to increased production of bilirubin, increased reabsorption of urobilinogen from the colon, or decreased hepatic clearance of urobilinogen.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Pathophysiology -- HEPATIC
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatocellular uptake - The bilirubin released from the reticuloendothelial system is in an unconjugated form (i.e., non-soluble) and gets transported to the hepatocytes bound to albumin which accomplishes solubility in blood. The albumin-bilirubin bond is broken, and the bilirubin alone is then taken into the hepatocytes through a carrier-membrane transport and bound to proteins in the cytosol to decrease the efflux of bilirubin back into the plasma. Conjugation of bilirubin - This unconjugated bilirubin then proceeds to the endoplasmic reticulum, where it undergoes conjugation to glucuronic acid resulting in the formation of conjugated bilirubin, which is soluble in the bile. This is rendered by the action of UDP-glucuronosyl transferase.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pathologic elevation of conjugated or direct bilirubin (concentration higher than 2 mg/dL or more than 20% of total bilirubin) is termed conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. [1] It is a biochemical marker of cholestasis and hepatocellular dysfunction. [1] Approximately 80% of the bilirubin is derived from hemoglobin metabolism. [2] The breakdown of heme molecules in hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochromes, catalase, tryptophan pyrrolase, and peroxidase results in the production of the catabolic product, bilirubin. [2] Heme is converted into biliverdin, which is further reduced to unconjugated bilirubin. [3] Subsequently, unconjugated bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronate to create conjugated bilirubin in the liver. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is linked to several serious neonatal complications. It includes نزف سري بعد الولادة (intracranial hemorrhage after birth) and نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage) as direct manifestations of the hemorrhagic component. In addition, the condition is associated with يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). The neonatal jaundice observed in this context is specifically described as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation and further clarified as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, indicating that the immature hepatic conjugation pathway in the newborn leads to accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin. Recognizing the interplay between hemorrhagic events, respiratory bleeding, and delayed‑conjugation jaundice is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: and perinatal jaundice are evaluated by total hemoglobin concentration, and laboratory values below reference ranges support their diagnosis. Neonatal jaundice manifests in multiple specific forms: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), nuclear jaundice (يرقان نووي), nuclear jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي) and physiologic neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). These jaundice types arise from hemolysis, delayed bilirubin conjugation, nutritional factors (breast‑milk) or birth‑timing complications, and they frequently co‑occur with hemorrhagic events. Hemorrhagic disorders anchored in the Neonatal period include loss of umbilical cord clamp leading to bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is a specific form of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This condition is characterized by a spectrum of related neonatal complications. Among them are neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, which results from a delayed development of the conjugating system, and the broader manifestation described as يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). In addition, affected infants may experience نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ, a hemorrhage of the respiratory tract in the newborn. Together, these interrelated features underscore the importance of recognizing Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome within the context of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease, as they impact bilirubin metabolism, bleeding risk, and overall newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) serves as a broad category that includes several distinct neonatal conditions. Within this category, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation is recognized as a specific form of jaundice that manifests in the newborn period. Additional conditions that occur in the neonatal timeframe include نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic or nuclear jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities illustrate the variety of etiologies—ranging from delayed bilirubin conjugation and breast‑milk factors to prematurity and respiratory bleeding—that can affect newborns, underscoring the importance of recognizing each specific presentation for accurate diagnosis and appropriate neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified within the broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This category also encompasses several distinct forms of neonatal jaundice. One form is neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation that results from the delayed development of the bilirubin‑conjugating system. Additional subtypes include breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), jaundice that arises from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and jaundice that is associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these conditions represent various manifestations of impaired bilirubin metabolism and hemolysis in the newborn period, highlighting the importance of recognizing specific etiologies for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1199
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier encompasses several distinct clinical entities. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system represents a form of early‑life hyperbilirubinemia caused by the immature hepatic capacity to process bilirubin. In addition, neonatal hemorrhage after birth (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ) are recognized as bleeding complications that can occur in the immediate post‑natal period, affecting either the umbilical region or the respiratory passages. Another neonatal manifestation is nuclear jaundice resulting from the protective coating (التمنيع الإسوي) in embryos or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), which reflects a specific type of jaundice linked to fetal or neonatal physiological factors. Together, these entities illustrate the range of disorders and complications that are characteristic of the neonatal stage, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and management of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory conditions in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1097
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) is associated with a loose umbilical cord clamp that can lead to bleeding, and it occurs in several neonatal conditions including neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). It is also found in neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), particularly the physiologic form that results from immature bilirubin metabolism in fetuses or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), as well as in jaundice that is linked to preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). These interconnections highlight the range of complications that can be present in the neonatal period and underscore the importance of careful monitoring and management of umbilical cord handling, respiratory health, and bilirubin levels in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2344
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is categorized within the broader condition of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn encompasses several related pediatric entities, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interconnected disorders illustrate the diverse mechanisms—such as impaired bilirubin conjugation, respiratory tract bleeding, milk‑related bilirubin excess, and prematurity‑related metabolic immaturity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic presentations in the newborn, underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically occur in the fetal and/or neonatal period and are closely related to the Arabic‑language concept “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice). Evaluation of these jaundice types relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, which is used to interpret the clinical significance of the findings. In this context, a laboratory result that falls below the reference range is interpreted as an important indicator when assessing hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2424
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a composite neonatal condition that encompasses both hemorrhagic and hemolytic complications. Among its manifestations are neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation of bilirubin, which results from the delayed development of the hepatic conjugating system and reflects physiologic immaturity of the newborn’s liver (described in Arabic as “يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد”). The condition also includes umbilical bleeding that can occur when the umbilical cord clamp is loose, referred to in Dutch as “losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding” and in Arabic as “نزف سري بعد الولادة”. Together, these child entities illustrate that NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN involves both impaired bilirubin metabolism leading to jaundice and bleeding complications related to umbilical cord management, highlighting the need for careful monitoring of liver function and cord care in affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is classified as a neonatal condition. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is used to denote a range of disorders that occur in the newborn period, including several distinct types of neonatal jaundice: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory distress), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑associated jaundice). These interrelated neonatal entities share the common context of early‑life physiology and pathology, underscoring the importance of recognizing their overlapping presentations for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in newborn care.
